Center: US National Nuclear Data Center, Brookhaven, USA
ref: Jour: Physical Review, Part C, Nuclear Physics, Vol.113, p.025807 (2026)
title: Measurement of the 92Mo(p,gamma)93Tc reaction cross section for the gamma-process nucleosynthesis

x4freetext: Measurement of the 92Mo(p,gamma)93Tc reaction cross
section for the gamma-process nucleosynthesis

x4codes.shortRef: Jour: Physical Review, Part C, Nuclear Physics, Vol.113, p.025807 (2026)

x4freetext: Main publication.
Data and technical details.

x4codes.hlp: University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, IN, United States of America
University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, IN, United States of America

x4freetext: The 5U accelerator at the Nuclear
Science Laboratory at the University of Notre Dame.
The beam pipe surrounding the target was used as a
Faraday cup and was used to monitor the beam current
throughout the experiment. The beam current ranged
from a maximum of 1 microA at low energies to 10 nA
at high energies. At higher energies the beam current
was chosen to maximize the count rate while
minimizing the detector dead time, while at lower
energies the beam current was chosen to maximize
count rate while ensuring target stability. The
uncertainty in the center-of-mass energy comes from a
combination of the uncertainty in the beam energy
resolution, 1-2 keV, and the energy loss through the
target, 40-80 keV.
The High EfficienCy TOtal absorption
spectrometeR (HECTOR). HECTOR is a NaI(Tl) array
16x16x16 in. in size divided into 16 segments, each
read by two photomultipliers. The segments are
arranged into a cube with a 60-mm borehole through
its center to fit a 2-in. beam pipe.
Two different 92Mo targets were
used, with a thickness of 836(93) and 1070(120)
microg/cm2. The thinner target was used to achieve a
higher energy resolution to study the resonance
structures and the thicker target was used at the
lowest energies to increase statistics. Both targets
were 99.93% enriched, and were evaporated onto a
96-mg/cm2 gold backing to stop the beam at the target
location and reduce the beam induced background.
The measurements lasted a few minutes to 3 h to allow
for sufficient statistics in the sum peak. A total of
350 energy points were measured during the
experiment. The gamma rays from the deexcitation
cascade following the proton capture were detected in
HECTOR. The large angular coverage of HECTOR allowed
for the use of the gamma-summing technique. When the
93Tc that is produced during the reaction deexcites
to the ground state, it emits a series of gamma rays,
which form a cascade. Within HECTOR, all of the gamma
rays emitted in the cascade are summed into a sum
peak. The energy of the sum peak is given by ESum =
Ec.m. + Q - Ef, where Ec.m. is the center-of-mass
energy of the system; Q is the Q value of the
reaction, which in this case is 4.086 MeV; and Ef is
the energy of the final state of the nucleus. In the
case discussed here, there are two sum peaks present
in the spectrum, one for events that deexcite to the
ground state and one for events that deexcite to the
isomeric state at 392 keV. The isomeric state has a
half-life of 43 min, thus it does not deexcite to the
ground state within the light collection time of the
crystals, leading to the distinct peak in the sum
spectrum.
Approved by A.Simon on March 31, 2026.
BP
The reaction cross section obtained in this work is
given by sigma = NS/(Nb*nt*epsilonS), where NS is
the number of counts in the sum peak, Nb is the total
number of beam particles, nt is the areal target
density, and epsilonS is the summing efficiency.
Total uncertainty.
up to 5% statistical uncertainty in the
number of events in the sum peak.
a 5% uncertainty in the number of beam
particles.
About 10% uncertainty in the target thickness
for each target.
A relative uncertainty of 5-20% in the
summing efficiency.
Repeatedly measured data point.
Table A.2

x4freetext: The reaction rate is given by NA<sigmav> =
sqrt(8/(pi*mu))*((1/kT)**(3/2))*0toinfinity Integral
E*sigma(E)*exp(-E/kT) *dE, where NA is Avogadro`s
constant, mu is the reduced mass, kT is the thermal
energy, E is the center-of-mass energy, and sigma(E)
is the corresponding total cross section.

x4code.headerHelp: Error in value of quantity, defined under ERR-ANALYS

x4freetext: The uncertainty for this work, shown in
light red, was calculated by performing a Monte Carlo
sampling of the cross section from a Gaussian
distribution, where the mean and standard deviation
were equal to the measured cross section and its
uncertainty, respectively.
